title: The Coordination Tax: Light-Speed Delay in a Self-Replicating Probe Swarm Bites Only When Hops Are Long
abstract: A swarm of self-replicating probes fills a stellar field star by star, each probe settling a star and launching copies. The standard exploration-timescale model grants every probe perfect, instantaneous knowledge of which stars are already settled, and names finite light-speed as its own future work. We add that light-speed limit: a probe deciding at a star treats a distant star as settled only once the news, travelling at the speed of light, has arrived, so probes race for the same target from stale views and waste trips. Over a 32-seed paired ensemble on identical galaxies, the delay slows the time to fill the field by a median of about 0 percent for powered nearest-neighbour flight, about 30 percent (95 percent bootstrap CI 24 to 38) for nearest-star slingshot flight, and about 51 percent (47 to 54) for maximum-boost slingshot flight; the slingshot penalties are positive in all 32 seeds, hold across coverage fractions from a quarter of the field to all of it, and persist over a four-fold increase in field size. Every case still reaches full coverage, so lag alone leaves no permanent unsettled fraction. The ratio of information delay to travel time across a hop equals the probe speed in units of the speed of light, and it gates whether the delay can bite at all: at the powered cruise speed it is negligible. But because that ratio is the same on every hop, it does not predict the penalty, which instead tracks how non-local each hop is: the two slingshot policies reach comparable speeds, yet the one that reaches past its neighbours to distant stars makes wasted trips about twice as long and pays about seventy percent more. Fast-but-local flight is nearly immune; the cost falls on the long-range hops that made the swarm fast. Under finite light-speed the slingshot speed-up is real, but a meaningful fraction of it is eaten by uncoordinated long-range collisions.
